summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces Packer, an open source tool for creating machine images across multiple platforms from a single template. Packer addresses the gap between powerful machine image concepts and the tooling needed for modern cloud workflows. The tool can generate AMIs, VirtualBox images, and Vagrant boxes in parallel, enabling use cases like continuous delivery pipelines and synchronized production/development environments. While version 0.1 has limitations, Hashimoto draws parallels to Vagrant's early days, expressing confidence the project will grow with community involvement.
key_points: Packer uses a single portable template format to generate machine images for multiple platforms simultaneously
Existing tooling for machine images hasn't kept pace with modern cloud infrastructure's agile workflow demands
Key use cases include automated AMI creation for continuous delivery and creating nearly identical production/development environments
The project follows the same community-driven growth model as Vagrant, which started with significant limitations that were removed over time
money_quotes: Machine images are a powerful concept, but the existing tooling around them hasn't caught up to the agile workflow that modern cloud infrastructures enable.
Packer uses a single portable input format -- a template -- to generate the images for multiple platforms in parallel.
Packer is a general purpose image building tool. The opportunities to build things around it are endless.
When I released version 0.1 of Vagrant, it only worked on Mac, only worked with VirtualBox, only ran Ubuntu machines, and only worked with serverless Chef.
As the Vagrant community grew so did its capabilities, and now all those limitations have been removed.
key_insight: A single portable template that generates identical machine images across multiple platforms closes the gap between infrastructure-as-code ideals and practical DevOps workflows.
